coredata.dc:title: Modeling and simulation of the thermodynamics of lithium-ion battery intercalation materials in the open-source software Cantera

coredata.dc:creator: Mayur, Manik
DeCaluwe, Steven C.
Kee, Benjamin L.
Bessler, Wolfgang G.
coredata.dc:description: Modeling and simulation play a key role in analyzing the complex electrochemical behavior of lithium-ion batteries. We present the development of a thermodynamic and kinetic modeling framework for intercalation electrochemistry within the open-source software Cantera. Instead of using equilibrium potentials and single-step Butler-Volmer kinetics, Cantera is based on molar thermodynamic data and mass-action kinetics, providing a physically-based and flexible means for complex reaction pathways. Herein, we introduce a new thermodynamic class for intercalation materials into the open-source software. We discuss the derivation of molar thermodynamic data from experimental half-cell potentials, and provide practical guidelines. We then demonstrate the new class using a single-particle model of a lithium cobalt oxide/graphite lithium-ion cell, implemented in MATLAB. With the present extensions, Cantera provides a platform for the lithium-ion battery modeling community both for consistent thermodynamic and kinetic models and for exchanging the required thermodynamic and kinetic parameters. We provide the full MATLAB code and parameter files as supplementary material to this article.

head.ce:title: Modeling and simulation of the thermodynamics of lithium-ion battery intercalation materials in the open-source software Cantera

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Energy Systems Technology (INES), Offenburg University of Applied Sciences, Badstrasse 24, 77652, Offenburg, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Energy Systems Technology (INES), Offenburg University of Applied Sciences, Badstrasse 24, 77652 Offenburg, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical Engineering, Colorado School of Mines, Golden, CO, 80401, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mechanical Engineering, Colorado School of Mines, Golden, Colorado 80401, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modeling and simulation play a key role in analyzing the complex electrochemical behavior of lithium-ion batteries. We present the development of a thermodynamic and kinetic modeling framework for intercalation electrochemistry within the open-source software Cantera. Instead of using equilibrium potentials and single-step Butler-Volmer kinetics, Cantera is based on molar thermodynamic data and mass-action kinetics, providing a physically-based and flexible means for complex reaction pathways. Herein, we introduce a new thermodynamic class for intercalation materials into the open-source software. We discuss the derivation of molar thermodynamic data from experimental half-cell potentials, and provide practical guidelines. We then demonstrate the new class using a single-particle model of a lithium cobalt oxide/graphite lithium-ion cell, implemented in MATLAB. With the present extensions, Cantera provides a platform for the lithium-ion battery modeling community both for consistent thermodynamic and kinetic models and for exchanging the required thermodynamic and kinetic parameters. We provide the full MATLAB code and parameter files as supplementary material to this article.

ce:section.ce:para: The lithium-ion battery is an outstanding example for a complex reaction-transport system. Energy conversion takes place through a series of thermo-electro-chemical reactions happening in the bulk or at the interfaces of electrode and electrolyte materials. Mass, charge and heat transport take place over a wide range of spatial and temporal scales. Modeling and simulation play a key role in analyzing, understanding, and optimizing this complex system [1–3]. In particular, because experimental characterization of basic battery processes is challenging, physicochemical models with detailed considerations of thermo-electro-chemical processes during battery operation are necessary to understand and address many technical challenges in battery development [4].

A lithium-ion battery works on the principle of reversible storage and shuttling of lithium between cathode and anode active materials. Such a process, commonly known as intercalation (insertion of lithium in the host active material) [5] or de-intercalation (extraction of lithium from the host active material), leads to a change in the internal energy (enthalpy) and configuration (entropy) of the host material. The thermodynamics (enthalpy and entropy) of such a process are consequently a function of the concentration of lithium present in the host material and evolve during the charge-transfer reaction in a lithium-ion battery [6]. Although the thermodynamics can be identified experimentally for individual electrode materials [7–10], there is so far no standard workflow for implementing this information into computational tools, neither from the modeling perspective nor from the practical viewpoint of parameter processing.

Cantera [11] is an open-source simulation toolkit that provides interfaces for modeling complex chemical reaction systems and their underlying thermodynamics, kinetics and transport. For over 15 years it has emerged as a useful tool within a fast-growing community and is applied to a wide variety of problems, including combustion, detonations, fuel cells, batteries, plasmas, heterogeneous catalysis, and thin-film deposition. It includes functionality for homogeneous and heterogeneous phases, electro-chemical three phase boundaries, and combinations thereof. Cantera is an open-source alternative to commercial codes like CHEMKIN [12] and DETCHEM [13]. It provides object-oriented modules and library functions that can be used either from scripting software like Python and MATLAB, or applications written in C++ and Fortran 90. Cantera has been actively used to model thermodynamics and multi-step kinetics in batteries and fuel cells [14–21]. The use of Cantera simplifies the simulation procedure by automating the required thermodynamic, transport, and chemical kinetic calculations, and by standardizing the required input parameter processing. Although Cantera is well positioned as an electrochemical modeling tool set, its application to lithium-ion battery simulations has thus far been limited. One key reason for this limited use has been a paucity of phase classes available for accurately modeling the thermodynamics intercalation materials as a function of varying lithium content.

In this work, we describe the development and implementation of a thermodynamic model of intercalation materials used in lithium-ion batteries. We create a new Cantera thermodynamic class, BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo, to model non-ideal, concentration-dependent species thermodynamics. The new functionality is demonstrated by using a representative MATLAB based single particle model [22–24] of a graphite/lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO2, LCO) lithium-ion battery. Simulation results are compared to the results of Guo et al. [24] that follow an empirical approach for modeling half-cell and full-cell potentials from the experimental data. With the present extensions, Cantera provides a platform for the lithium-ion battery modeling community both for exchanging parameters and for consistent thermodynamic and kinetic models. The full MATLAB code and parameter files are provided as supplementary material to this article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Half-cell thermodynamics of an intercalation material
ce:section.ce:para:
An intercalation material used in lithium-ion batteries can be chemically described as two-species system, consisting of intercalated lithium atoms and vacancies in the host material [6,28], which we denote in the following as Li[AM] and V[AM], respectively, where AM stands for active material. Here and throughout, we use square brackets to denote the bulk phase hosting the species. In the nomenclature Li[AM] and V[AM] we omit partial charges that may be present on the intercalated lithium atom or in the host matrix, as common in lithium-ion battery literature (e.g., formulating
LiCoO
2
instead of
Li
+
Co
3 +
O
2
2 −
), for clarity and generality.

The electrochemical reaction at the active material surface is usually described as single-step charge-transfer reaction that involves species in three phases, namely, lithium ion in the electrolyte phase (
Li
+
[ elyt ]
), Li[AM] and V[AM] in the active material phase, and electron in an electrically conductive charge carrier ‘electrode’ phase (
e
−
[ elde ]
), for which the most commonly used material in battery cathodes is graphite. The intercalation reaction thus is generally given as,
(21)

Appendix 7.1); the definition of both may be different for different phases (here: electrolyte, electrode, and active material). Note that activity concentration and standard concentration of the electron formally enter the expression. Practically, Cantera uses the metal thermodynamics class for electrons, for which these concentrations are set to unity (cf. Appendix 7.1) and cancel out, providing a physically correct description of electron activity in an electronic conductor. Therefore, this term is taken out in the following equations. ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical guidelines for obtaining molar thermodynamics from experimental data
ce:section.ce:para:
One of the practical challenges for obtaining reliable half-cell thermodynamics (
h
Li
[ AM ]
0 , tab
and
s
Li
[ AM ]
0 , tab
) is to find self-consistent sources of the
E
and
d E / d T
data as function of intercalation stoichiometry
X
Li
[ AM ]
. Table 1 shows the list of some experimentally reported half-cell data for LCO. These data pose several challenges for their use as source for thermodynamic data:
•
The lower stoichiometry range is different for the different studies (
X
Li
= 0.25 … 0.48
), and not all studies report
d E / d T
.
•
The assignment of stoichiometries is different for the different studies. For example, upper voltages are 4.35 V at
X
Li
= 0.43 and 4.3 V at
X
Li
= 0.4 [30], which is inconsistent with the thermodynamic requirement that lower stoichiometries show higher voltages. A stoichiometry of 1.0 is assigned to different lower voltages ranging from 3.2 V to 3.85 V.
•
According to thermodynamics, stoichiometries of 0 or 1 should result in voltages of
+ ∞
and
− ∞
, respectively (cf. Eq. (23)). Assigning stoichiometries of 0 or 1 to finite voltages is thermodynamically inconsistent.
•
All studies are carried out without reference electrode. The reported voltages therefore may include contributions from kinetic overpotentials.

In order to obtain meaningful molar thermodynamics (enthalpy and entropy) data from published half-cell measurements, we suggest the following steps.
1.
Extract the
X
Li
[ AM ]
,
E
eq
and
X
Li
[ AM ]
,
d
E
eq
/ d T
tables from the reported half-cell data. (a) Make sure that both discharge and charge branches are shown, and use the average in order to avoid influences from kinetic overpotentials. (b) Use a sufficient number of data points in order to provide smooth curves even in ranges of strong gradients (as associated with phase changes in active materials). If required, the data can be distributed non-equidistant on the
X
Li
[ AM ]
scale. (c) If the
E
eq
and
d
E
eq
/ d T

Li
[ AM ]
scale. (d) If
d
E
eq
/ d T
are not available, use a value of 0 and note that simulations will be incorrect for varying temperature.
2.
The limiting stoichiometries of 0 and 1 lead to singularities in Eqs. (24) and (41). Although lithium-ion battery electrodes are typically balanced sufficiently away from these limits, they may be reached upon overcharge or overdischarge of the cell, or during extrapolation steps of a numerical solver. Therefore, for numerical stability of simulations, make the following manipulations of the data: (a) Do not use data at
X
Li
[ AM ]
= 1
or

[ AM ]
= 0

Li
[ AM ]

Li
[ AM ]

3.
Use Eqs. (38) and (39) to calculate the molar thermodynamics

As mentioned above, for the present study, we take experimental data by Kumaresan et al. (last row of Table 1) as primary data source. The CTI file obtained from these data using the guidelines from this Section is provided as supplementary material (cf. Appendix 7.4). It should be mentioned that the use of other primary data (other rows in Table 1) would result in different tabulated parameters and different resulting simulated cell voltages. The reason for selecting the Kumaresan et al. data is that they were also used in the modeling study by Guo et al. [24], thus allowing a direct comparison to that study (cf. Section 4). Users may easily create parameters using other available data, including different electrode materials, following the present guidelines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implementation of concentration-dependent thermodynamics in Cantera

ce:section.ce:para:
We implement the thermodynamic model described above in a new Cantera class BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo. Essentially, it implements Eqs. (40) and (41) based on user-provided tables for molar enthalpy and entropy as function of intercalation stoichiometry. The new class is a child of the existing class IdealSolidSolnPhase from which it inherits all state equations except the concentration-dependent thermodynamics of one species. In particular, density is calculated from molar volumes via
(42)

∑
i

i

where,

i

i
. This describes an ideal solution in the sense that partial molar volumes are equal to the pure species molar volumes. Additionally, the molar volumes are independent of temperature and pressure, therefore the phase is incompressible. In the context of lithium-ion intercalation materials, the state equation (42) allows to describe volume expansion upon intercalation through the parameter
Ω
i
. The activity concentration
c
i
and standard concentration
c
i
0
can have different definitions according to user selection (cf. Appendix 7.1); in the example provided below, we chose

i
=
X
i
and
c
i
0
= 1
(both dimensionless). Note that, when using this phase for modeling an intercalation material, the total number of constituent species is limited to two, viz. a lithium-deficient host species (or vacancy) and a lithium-intercalated host species.

At the time of publication, this class is implemented in the Cantera development branch following the release version 2.4, and can be retrieved from the Cantera GitHub repository [34]. The new class is to be included in a future stable release of the software. The implementation detail of the class is briefly discussed in Appendix 7.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: User syntax on CTI file level
ce:section.ce:para: The Cantera input data and parameters are typically provided in a Cantera input (CTI) file, with a specific format for defining thermodynamics phases, species properties, reaction interfaces, and reactions. The CTI file is relatively easy to read and create, but is internally converted via a Python code to an XML format for improved machine readability which is finally parsed by the Cantera C++ functions to extract the relevant information for Cantera.

An example of the definition of the anode intercalation phase on the CTI file level using the new thermodynamics class is shown in Fig. 3 . The phase declaration must have the following parameters defined: phase name, constituent elements, constituent species, and name of the species with tabulated thermodynamics. The table may include the units; if they are not included, global standard units are assumed. The species definition must include name, atomic composition, and molar volume. Note that, for this example, we set the molar volume according to
Ω
i
=
M
i
/ ρ
, representing a constant-density compound. Note also that the vacancy species includes the host lattice, which is a requirement for mass conservation and correct density calculation.

In order to demonstrate lithium-ion battery electrochemistry with Cantera, we choose the example of a single-particle model of a graphite/LCO lithium-ion cell. In particular, we reproduce here the model and simulation results of Guo et al. [24] and thus compare the present model with a published model. The key difference of these two models is the way to describe half-cell thermodynamics. Guo et al. apply empirical analytical expressions for
E
eq
and
d
E
eq
/ d T
, combining polynomial, rational and trigonometric functions in lengthy mathematical expressions. The present work instead uses tabulated molar enthalpies and entropies
h
Li
[ AM ]
0 , tab
and
s
Li
[ AM ]
0 , tab
. This has several advantages, (1) a higher flexibility with respect to different materials, (2) a chemical (instead of electrical) and therefore more fundamental representation of the materials, (3) a simpler, yet likely more precise parameterization from experimental half-cell data. The motivation for the comparison of model against model is to demonstrate the new Cantera class and to provide a verified hands-on example for future users. Note that Guo et al. have validated their model against experimental discharge curves at different temperatures and C-rates.

For the present study, the single-particle transport equations are implemented in MATLAB, while electrochemistry is calculated by Cantera via the Cantera-MATLAB interface. Fig. 4 shows the modeling domain that consists of an electrode pair (anode-separator-cathode) of a lithium-ion battery. Each electrode is assumed to be represented by a single, spherically-shaped active material particle. Inside the particle, lithium transport is modeled via Fickian diffusion. At the particle surface, charge transfer is assumed to take place in a single-step reaction. Each electrode is also assumed to contains an electronic conductor phase that provides/consumes electrons, which is modeled in 0D (isopotential), and which may the intercalation material itself (upon sufficient electronic conductivity). A spatially unresolved (0D) electrolyte phase is considered across the electrodes and the separator, contributing to the internal cell resistance with an ohmic resistance. The model is assumed isothermal at 25 °C.

Lithium transport in the particles is modeled by Fickian diffusion in spherical coordinates as,

For the two active materials, the new BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo class, as presented in Section 3, is used. The electrolyte is defined as IdealSolidSolnPhase and the electron conductor as metal phase. Each phase is characterized by its temperature, pressure, electric potential

(reported by Guo at 25 °C and open circuit) because we were not able to get meaningful values from the expressions reported by Guo et al.

Fig. 5 shows the simulation approach used in this work. For the sake of demonstrating the present framework, we use a sequential approach which decouples transport and electrochemistry. Firstly, the MATLAB partial differential equation solver pdepe is used to solve the transport Eq. (43)–(45), resulting in the radial distribution of lithium concentration in the particles as a function of time. Secondly, the lithium mole fraction at the particle surface,
X
Li , j
surf
is used to obtain cell potentials using a Newton solver and Cantera functionality. The details of the approach are discussed in the following.

The MATLAB pdepe solver is generally used for a system of PDEs for which the time and length scales are the same. Because our transport equations are at the particle scale, different particle sizes in the anode and cathode lead to a variable length scale problem for setting up the boundary conditions (cf. Eq. (45)). In order to resolve this problem, we non-dimensionalize the governing equations and boundary conditions, by rescaling particle radius and solver time with characteristic length and time scales. Here, the natural choice for the length scale of each electrode was the respective particle radius,
r
i
. For the time, the dimensional factor is taken as,
τ = 1

The electronic and ionic potentials are calculated as follows (cf. Fig. 5). First we set

Fig. 6 shows the comparison of the cell potential as obtained from our single-particle model and Guo's empirical model at different C-rates. The discharge rate of C/33 demonstrates a system in a quasi-equilibrium state, throughout the slow discharge process. Most of the half-cell thermodynamics data is reported at very slow C-rates in order to minimize the effect of current induced concentration gradients in the experiment setup. The cell potential at C/2 and 1C highlight the overpotential associated with the activation and ohmic losses in the cell. The simulation results of this work are similar, however not identical to the one obtained by Guo et al. The observed minor deviations in the cell discharge curves can be attributed to the fact Guo's model used empirical mathematical expressions for half-cell equilibrium potential
E
and
d E / d T
fitted to experimental data, while our model uses tabulated thermodynamics extracted from experimental data by Kumaresan et al. [29] as discussed in Section 2.5.

Fig. 7 shows the lithium stoichiometry distribution in cathode and anode particle at 1C discharge rates. The slow diffusion results in considerable concentration gradients. The concentration at the particle surface is passed to Cantera for calculating the thermodynamics and kinetics of the charge-transfer reaction.

Simulations at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 8 . The results show how decreasing temperature leads to increasing overpotentials (lower voltages) and, hence, lower capacities. The temperature dependence arises from both the electrochemical reactions (activation overpotentials) and the solid-state transport in the active materials (concentration overpotentials).

The present model is isothermal, yet can be applied to different temperatures. For a full thermal model, heat sources within the cell need to be quantified. This is straightforward with Cantera, as all required thermodynamic and kinetic information is included. As derived in the Appendix of Ref. [20], the heat source
Q ˙
chem
due an interfacial reaction can be given generally as,
(55)
Q ˙
chem
= r S
( − Δ H + F
ν
e
Δ ϕ )
.

This includes both reversible and irreversible heat contributions and is applicable to both, electrochemical reactions and thermochemical (non-charge-transfer) reactions (as in the latter case, the stoichiometric coefficient of the electron
ν
e
= 0
). The heat source
Q ˙
ohm
due to ohmic heating in the electrolyte is given as,
(56)
Q ˙

=
R
cell
I
app
2
.

The heat sources quantified such are shown in Fig. 9 . The contribution of the two electrodes are highly nonlinear and even show a change in sign. Note that the heat sources combine both, reversible and irreversible (entropic) heat contributions. The contribution of the electrolyte is constant, because both electrolyte resistance and discharge current are assumed constant. In case of thermal models, the calculated sum would enter the energy conservation equation as source term.

The results demonstrate the capability of the new Cantera BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo thermodynamic class to predict lithium-ion battery behavior. Although the simple single-particle model is based on considerable simplifications in comparison to full pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) or pseude-three-dimensional (P3D) models, it is sufficient as example for the MATLAB/Cantera simulation framework.

It is illustrative to compare the results from using tabulated thermodynamics with those that would be obtained from assuming an ideal intercalation material. The equilibrium cell voltage of a lithium-ion cell is given by,

are the concentration-dependent standard Gibbs energy for cathode and anode respectively. This equation shows the ideal concentration dependence of the equilibrium cell potential from the second term on the right-hand side, and the non-ideal excess part from the first term on the right-hand side. The realization of the ideal model with Cantera is straightforward by changing to the IdealSolidSolution class, which is the parent class of the new BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo class. The relevant part of the input file is shown in Fig. 10 . Note that, in comparison to Fig. 3, the tabulated values are omitted; instead, constant molar thermodynamic data are given in the species definition (here using the values of the table corresponding to 100% SOC).

Fig. 11 shows a comparison of equilibrium cell voltage with concentration-dependent excess thermodynamics (BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo, Fig. 3) and constant thermodynamics (IdealSolidSolution, Fig. 10). In order to obtain the equilibrium cell voltage at constant thermodynamics, the concentration dependent standard Gibbs energy,

were fixed to a value corresponding to a specific SOC, viz. 50% and 100%. The SOC vs. lithium stoichiometry was obtained from a C/33 discharge simulation. It can be observed that constant standard-state thermodynamics are not able to capture the equilibrium voltage variation of the LCO-graphite cell model, thus motivating the introduction of tabulated thermodynamics.

The open-source software Cantera was extended by a new thermodynamics class BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo that implements concentration-dependent molar enthalpies and entropies in an otherwise ideal solid solution phase. This class enables accurate thermodynamic modeling of intercalation materials and therefore expands the applicability of Cantera to the field of lithium-ion batteries. The required theoretical background was derived and discussed, and implementation details were given.

Many published lithium-ion battery models apply empirical analytical expressions for the half-cell potential
E
(
X
Li
)
and its temperature dependence
d E / d T
(
X
Li
)
. These expressions often combine polynomial, rational and/or trigonometric functions in lengthy mathematical equations. The present work instead uses tabulated molar enthalpies and entropies
h
Li
[ AM ]
0 , tab
and
s
Li
[ AM ]
0 , tab
as function of
X
Li
. This has several advantages, (1) a higher flexibility with respect to different materials, (2) a chemical (instead of electrical) and therefore more fundamental representation of the materials properties, (3) a simpler, yet likely more precise parameterization from experimental half-cell data. We have demonstrated that incorporating such tabulated thermodynamic data to represent non-ideal species interactions enables battery discharge curve predictions which are inaccessible using standard, ideal thermodynamic models, and matching previous empirically-based simulations.

For practical use, we have developed guidelines to obtain molar thermodynamics from experimentally-available
E
and
d E / d T
data. This is associated with several pitfalls, including inconsistent literature data and the need to correct for the entropy of the lithium-metal counter electrode. With the help of a MATLAB based single-particle model, we have successfully demonstrated the applicability of the new thermodynamic class. The code and input files are provided as supplementary material to this article.

The strength of Cantera's electrochemical framework is its flexibility. It is based on a fundamental thermodynamic and mass-action kinetic framework, which implicitly includes Butler-Volmer and Nernst equations. As perspective, the new thermodynamic class can be used in any combination with other bulk phases and interfaces, arbitrary electro- and thermochemical reactions, and within complex reaction mechanisms. A current example is the simulation of ageing and high-temperature behavior of a lithium-ion cell, where additional primary and secondary solid electrolyte interface formation reactions are conveniently added [36].

Cantera can be coupled to transport models implemented in different languages, including MATLAB (as shown here), Python, and C++, providing the full thermochemical backbone (current, reaction rates, heat source terms) based on easily-accessible parameter input files. For example, Cantera was coupled before to the CFD code OpenFOAM in the context of turbulent flame simulations [35]. Therefore, Cantera can provide a platform for exchanging electrochemical models and their parameters within the lithium-ion battery modeling community.

The thermodynamic class BinarySolutionTabulatedThermo is a derived class of the existing thermodynamic class IdealSolidSolnPhase. The calculation of species activity and equation of state (required for phase density) is inherited from the parent class, but the calculation of molar thermodynamics (enthalpy and entropy) is overwritten. As in the parent class, different definitions of the standard concentration
c
i
0
can be selected. Further, the new thermodynamic class overwrites the _initThermoXML() method from the parent class, which is used to initialize the thermodynamic phase from an XML based Cantera input file. This method is called only once at the beginning of phase initialization and sets some global variables such as m_kk_tab, the index of intercalating species in the phase species list, m_xlast, the last mole fraction of the intercalation species, the experimentally obtained thermodynamics data in 3 arrays (C++: vector), viz. m_molefrac_tab for mole fraction, m_enthalpy_tab for molar enthalpy (J/(mol)), and m_entropy_tab for molar entropy (J/(mol·K)), and interpolation data, which are read from the XML file.

In the Cantera parent class, molar thermodynamics are temperature-dependent as calculated from the standard-state thermodynamics of constituent species. When the phase temperature changes, the molar thermodynamics of the constituent species are re-calculated via the method _updateThermo(). In the new derived class, the molar thermodynamics are additionally a function of the mole fraction of intercalating species. Consequently, apart from phase temperature, a change in the mole fraction of the intercalating species also invokes the method _updateThermo, which updates the standard state thermodynamics according to Eqs. (40) and (41),
